ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKP covers beef fillet/tenderloin grilled; lean-only trimming is not a separate facet code. Closest defensible match used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for grilled lean beef fillet as a food item; left null to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical single serving of grilled lean beef fillet is approximately 120 g, equivalent to a palm-sized portion of cooked meat.

nutrient_source.title: Beef, fillet, grilled, lean — Singapore HPB Foodo Food Composition Database (cr_id F06030027)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Input record cr_id F06030027 is sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo Food Composition Database with review_status reviewed and confidence 0.95. All core per_100g macro and micronutrient values are taken from this approved primary regional source without modification. Saturated_fat_g of 2.1 g/100 g is cross-referenced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy NDB 13365 (Beef, tenderloin, steak, separable lean only, trimmed to 0 inch fat, all grades, cooked, broiled) as HPB does not separately report saturated fat for this entry; this is consistent with total fat 5.5 g/100 g. Trans_fat_g of 0.1 g/100 g represents naturally occurring ruminant trans fat as reported in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for lean cooked beef tenderloin.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index not applicable — negligible carbohydrate content confirmed per HPB Singapore Foodo (F06030027) and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy NDB 13365
gi_source.organization: Health Promotion Board (HPB), Singapore; USDA FoodData Central
gi_source.source_match_notes: Grilled lean beef fillet contains 0.0 g carbohydrate per 100 g as confirmed by HPB Singapore Foodo (cr_id F06030027, approved primary source) and corroborated by USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy NDB 13365. Glycemic index is not applicable as this food contains negligible carbohydrates. The University of Sydney GI Database does not assign GI values to foods with negligible carbohydrate content. GL per serving is 0.0 by definition. glycemic_index is set to null (not 0) per Veritas critical rule for negligible-carbohydrate foods.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (49 mg/100 g) and meaningful potassium (350 mg/100 g) supporting a favourable Na:K ratio consistent with DASH dietary principles.
hypertension.guidance: Grilled lean beef fillet is suitable for hypertensive individuals when prepared without added salt or high-sodium sauces; limit portion to 100–150 g per meal and pair with potassium-rich vegetables to further support blood pressure control.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Zero carbohydrate and zero sugar content means no postprandial glucose load; high protein (31.9 g/100 g) supports satiety and lean body mass preservation.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Grilled lean beef fillet is an appropriate protein source for people with Type 2 Diabetes as it contributes no glycaemic load; avoid sweet marinades or glazes that would add carbohydrates, and pair with non-starchy vegetables and a high-fibre carbohydrate source for a balanced meal per ADA dietary guidelines.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat (~2.1 g/100 g) and cholesterol (70 mg/100 g) are present; trace trans fat (0.1 g/100 g ruminant origin) is noted, though total fat is low at 5.5 g/100 g for a lean cut.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Lean grilled beef fillet can be included in a heart-healthy diet in moderate portions (≤100 g, 3–4 times per week) as part of a diet low in saturated fat; choose dry-heat cooking without added fat and balance with oily fish, legumes, and plant proteins to meet AHA dietary targets for saturated fat below 6% of total energy.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Arachidonic acid in red meat can amplify pro-inflammatory cytokine pathways relevant to RA; trace ruminant trans fat (0.1 g/100 g) is pro-inflammatory.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should limit red meat including lean beef fillet to no more than 1–2 servings per week per EULAR dietary guidance, and preferentially replace with anti-inflammatory protein sources such as fatty fish rich in omega-3 fatty acids.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Grilled lean beef fillet is a high-protein, low-fat cooked meat product derived from the tenderloin portion of beef cattle. The tenderloin is one of the most tender cuts of beef, located along the spine, and is prized for its fine-grained texture and mild, clean beef flavour. For this preparation, all visible external and internal separable fat is removed before and after grilling, resulting in a lean muscle-meat product. The grilling process imparts a slightly charred exterior while retaining a juicy interior, and no added fats, oils, or sauces are used. Per 100 g, this food provides 178 kcal, 31.9 g protein, 5.5 g total fat (approximately 2.1 g saturated fat), 0 g carbohydrate, 0 g sugar, 49 mg sodium, 350 mg potassium, 10 mg calcium, 2.2 mg iron, and 70 mg cholesterol. The very high protein density makes it an excellent choice for muscle maintenance, weight management, and post-exercise recovery. The negligible carbohydrate content means it contributes no glycaemic load, making it suitable for low-carbohydrate, ketogenic, and diabetic dietary patterns. The low sodium content (49 mg/100 g) and favourable potassium level (350 mg/100 g) make it compatible with DASH dietary principles for blood pressure management. Clinically, it is appropriate for Type 2 Diabetes management, hypertension control, and general high-protein dietary needs, though individuals with cardiovascular disease or rheumatoid arthritis should moderate red meat intake in line with AHA and EULAR guidelines respectively. It is naturally gluten-free, dairy-free, and suitable for halal dietary requirements when sourced from halal-certified beef.
common_user_queries: How many calories in grilled lean beef fillet?
Is grilled beef fillet good for diabetes?
Is lean beef fillet high in protein?
Is grilled beef tenderloin suitable for hypertension?
How much fat is in lean grilled beef fillet?
What is the sodium content of grilled beef fillet?
Can I eat beef fillet if I have high blood pressure?
Is lean beef fillet good for weight loss?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo Food Composition Database (cr_id F06030027, review_status reviewed, confidence 0.95). HPB is an approved primary regional source. The food description precisely matches the HPB entry for grilled lean beef fillet. Saturated fat cross-referenced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (approved secondary global source), documented in nutrient_source notes.
notes: Carbohydrate_g is confirmed zero per HPB Singapore Foodo source (F06030027); grilled lean beef fillet is a pure muscle-meat product with no carbohydrate-containing ingredients — confirmed zero. Sugar_g is naturally zero as no sugars are present in lean grilled beef muscle meat. Dietary_fibre_g is naturally zero as this is an animal muscle-meat product containing no plant fibre. Saturated_fat_g of 2.1 g/100 g is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy NDB 13365 (Beef, tenderloin, steak, separable lean only, trimmed to 0 inch fat, all grades, cooked, broiled) as HPB does not separately report saturated fat for this entry; this is consistent with total fat of 5.5 g/100 g. Trans_fat_g of 0.1 g/100 g represents naturally occurring ruminant trans fat (vaccenic acid / conjugated linoleic acid) as reported for lean cooked beef tenderloin in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy. Monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown not separately reported in HPB Foodo for this entry. Vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: these micronutrients are not reported in the HPB Foodo entry for this food item; limited published data in HPB Foodo for this preparation as of 2026. Glycemic index is not applicable as this food contains negligible carbohydrates (0.0 g/100 g); gi_category set to not_applicable and glycemic_index set to null per Veritas critical rule. GL per serving is 0.0 by definition. The University of Sydney GI Database does not assign GI values to foods with negligible carbohydrate content. Halal suitability is conditional on beef being sourced from a halal-certified abattoir.

Identity is unambiguous.
